title: Generics
subtitle: Teach one class or method to work with any type — safely.
goal: Understand type parameters, write a generic class Box<T> and Pair<A,B>, write a generic method, use a bounded type <T extends Number>, and see why generics beat casting from Object.

blocks.heading: What are generics? 🧩
blocks.html:
Generics let you write a class or method with a blank for the type, then fill in the real type later. The blank is called a type parameter, and we usually name it with a single capital letter like T (for "Type").

You have actually used generics already! Every time you wrote List<String> or ArrayList<Integer>, the part inside the angle brackets < > told Java which type goes in the blank. Today you'll build your own generic things.

blocks.heading: Writing a generic class: Box<T>
blocks.html:
Imagine a box that can hold one thing. We don't want to write a separate StringBox, IntBox, and CatBox — that's silly. Instead we write Box<T> once, where T is a stand-in for whatever type we put in later.

When you create one, you pick the type: new Box<String>("hello") makes a box of String, and Java remembers that.

blocks.caption: One Box class, many types
blocks.explain: Box<T> uses T everywhere a normal class would name a real type. wordBox is a Box<String>, so get() returns a String — that's why we can call .length() on it with no casting. numBox is a Box<Integer>. One class, both work, fully type-safe.

blocks.html: The diamond <>: when you write Box<String> b = new Box<>("hi"), the empty <> on the right is the "diamond." Java already knows the type from the left side, so you don't have to repeat it.

blocks.heading: Generic methods and a Pair<A,B>
blocks.html:
A method can have its own type parameter too. You write the blank in angle brackets before the return type, like static <T> T firstOf(T[] items). Java figures out what T is from the argument you pass.

And a class can have more than one type parameter. A Pair<A, B> holds two values that can be different types — perfect for pairing a name with a number.

blocks.caption: A generic method plus a two-type Pair
blocks.output:
First name: Maya
First score: 90
Leo is 11
blocks.explain: firstOf works on a String[] AND an Integer[] — one method, any array type. Pair<A, B> uses two blanks: A for the key, B for the value. So Pair<String, Integer> keeps a String key and an Integer value, each given its own type.

blocks.heading: Bounded types: <T extends Number>
blocks.html:
Sometimes "any type" is too loose. If a method does math, the type must at least be a number. A bounded type says "T can be anything, as long as it's a Number" — written <T extends Number>.

Because Java now knows T is a Number, you're allowed to call Number methods like doubleValue() on it.

blocks.caption: Only numbers allowed
blocks.explain: <T extends Number> restricts T to Number and its children (Integer, Double, and friends). That's what lets us call a.doubleValue(). Try passing a String and the code wouldn't even compile — the bound blocks it before your program ever runs.

blocks.html: Why generics beat Object + casting: the old way was to store everything as Object and then cast it back, like (String) box.get(). If you guessed the wrong type, your program crashed at runtime. Generics catch that mistake while you're still writing the code, and you never have to cast. Safer and tidier.

blocks.question: In Box<T>, what is T?
options: A built-in Java type like int
A type parameter — a blank that gets filled with a real type later
blocks.explain: T is a type parameter: a placeholder for a type. When you write Box<String>, T becomes String for that box.

blocks.question: What does <T extends Number> add to a generic method?
options: It makes the method run faster
It limits T to Number (and its subtypes), so number methods are allowed
It turns T into a String
It means the method returns nothing
blocks.explain: It's a bound. T must be a Number or a subtype like Integer or Double, which is why you can safely call something like doubleValue().

blocks.question: Why are generics better than storing things as Object and casting?
options: Generics catch type mistakes at compile time, with no casting needed
Object is not a real Java type
Generics use less memory than any other approach
blocks.explain: With Object you cast and risk a runtime crash if you guess wrong. Generics check the type while you code and remove the casts entirely — safer and cleaner.

blocks.title: Fill in the Box
blocks.instructions: The Box<T> class is missing the body of get(). Make get() return the stored value. Then main prints what's inside. Press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Nice — your generic Box hands back exactly what you put in!
blocks.failMsg: Inside get(), write return value; so the method returns the stored item.

blocks.title: Make a Pair
blocks.instructions: In main, create a Pair<String, Integer> holding the key "apples" and the value 6, store it in a variable named p, then print it as shown. The Pair class is already written for you. Press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Great — a Pair holds two values of different types at once!
blocks.failMsg: Write Pair<String, Integer> p = new Pair<>("apples", 6); on its own line before the println.

challenge.title: A generic middle() method
challenge.instructions: Write a generic method middle that takes a T[] array and returns its middle element (use items[items.length / 2]). main already calls it on a String array and an Integer array. Fill in the method body. Press Check.
challenge.hint: The middle index of an array of length n is n / 2. So return items[items.length / 2]; — it works for any type T.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 One method, any array type, zero casting — that's the power of generics. Day 19 complete!
challenge.failMsg: Inside middle, return items[items.length / 2]; so it hands back the middle element no matter the type.
